ayoreo:
Mu
chojningame yu: “Atata gotodie ayoredie.” Mu chiese
ojninga: “Que yatata ore uje idája ore iji yu. Jé cachi ca araja ore íode.”
english: And*Ichajúidé*said: “I know you, you are*Dupade*’*s*Son, you are Jesus.”

ayoreo: *Jadia – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: *Jadia – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*

ayoreo: Yujátadé:                    Cutamurajane ore eduguéjna bajade uña.
english: *Yujátadé:  He was of the Cutamurajane Clan and was one of their first leaders.

ayoreo: (Catecã́ri Caratai)
english: *Acacomi Yui Caratac*

ayoreo: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 11 de octubre de 1985.*
english: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985.*

ayoreo:
Mu Jesus chi chudute ore uruode
ga chi cho quique enga chojninga: “Que cho ujetiga yicai jnumi tudé gu ujetiga
ucué erámi jogosórone ore ujetiga uome ore, ujetiga paraque ore. Yucuegode
udojo u uje gajnareque ore adode,” chi chojninga Jesus. Jé jno doquidode údi
enga chojninga: “Yucuegode u erámi jogosórone ore uje sijnaque ore. Yucué
sijnangone ore iji erámi.”
english: Take for example Zacchias, who we don’t know personally, except that he was a thief, and very sinful. Remember how Zacchias took The-great-one-we- listen-to, Jesus Christ, to his house, even though the people resented him for it. One of the people said, “How is it that people claim that Jesus in so good, but here he is eating with a sinful man.”

ayoreo: Jesus uruode u uje chingo yóguiji*San Juan 17*uje chi idógosi jeta jnome gaté nanique, ga qué catecã́ri dayé iji jnumi ome dacadigode ore. Ga chi chojninga iji dacatecãraitigade: “Yapá, a yijíñome yocurasade ore to iji jnumi, jnutiminórone. Icatecã́ri ua jeta arodipise ore iji jnumi to,” chojninga, “enga arodipise ore ga jé adi tu gajnerĩ uje ugúsi ore gari diríca ga yirodipise ore iji jnumi. Yingo baruode aja ore. Yingo baruode ojningane cucha aja ore, ga jé ore doi jnese. Yimate baroquéode iji jnumi te ga, a yajnésara uaque ga jé ca etaque uñeque.”
english: *Jesús catecã́ri Dayé ome dacadigode Ecarai* **prayed for his disciples when he was about to leave for heaven:** These are Jesus’s words that he tells us about in*John 17*when he would soon be going back to heaven; while he was still on earth he prayed to his Father for his disciples. This is what he prayed: “Father, I’m going to be leaving our countrymen here on earth, these earth dwellers. I ask you to take good care of them here on earth,” he said, “as I have watched over them while I have been here with them. I gave them your Word. And I explained what you said, and they have obeyed everything you told them to do.  I have finished the work you gave me to do here on earth, and now I’m returning to you. Don’t disregard any of them.”

ayoreo:
Ãro
yocurõsoringuéone iji yocúdode jnese, Dupade. Yocurajédie, yoquejnarec uñane,
Dupade, uje déji chequedíe, disiode ore, jnanione ore jnese, Dupade. Ãro
urõsoringuéone iji yocúdode jnese, Dupade. Etoguipise yoque, Dupade, jeta yico
dínique jnese, Dupade, ome yocúringai ayoré ijõre.
english: Get rid of the pain we have in our bodies,*Dupade*. Heal our coughs and the other illnesses we have,*Dupade*– do this for the women, the children, and the men, all of them. Erase the pain of all our bodies,*Dupade*. May we be strong,*Dupade,*so that we can face each day in good health.

ayoreo: Jé noña ga cabayui oidi ayoré noñome cuchiso iji toniñeque.
english: They say the tapir said: “Your words, what were they?”

ayoreo:
Dupade Uruode chatata uje iji
taningai icaite erámi taningane ga cuchisode parácho jnese icaite. Cuchisode
uñane chicai dasocade icaite iji isocade iji dirode unojo. Dupade Uruode
chatata uje Dupade chi pésu cuchiso ponáyone jnese. Chi parácho cuchisode jnese
icaite enga cuchisode chi ca asécho gotique icaite iji erámone taningane uje
qué Dupade déji Adã, Eba ore gari iji dejnai. Ugade chi parácho icaite.
Caratade chi parácho. Cuchisode chi ca asécho. Chi parácho jnese.
english: God’s Word says that in the beginning all the animals were tame. Some of the animals were just like they are today. The Bible tells us that God made all kinds of animals. And all of them were tame in the beginning and none of them were wild when Adam and Eve were still in the garden. Even the snakes were tame. The jaguars were tame. None of the animals were wild. All were tame.

ayoreo: Ore i uñai u:**Cutérepaogóronegosóde**
english: *Chabotodiegosode –*“People of the bats.”

ayoreo: Ga*cucujna*chojninga: “A yijína yajábi ga yipatíorango ñane ome gotique.”
english: *The gopher said: “I’ll stay with my dear brother the jaguar and whatever that thing does will happen to us both.”*

ayoreo: Jnuruminone – pajei carubode
english: They would shake their heads and make their tied-back hair move back and forth. They would paint themselves with the powder that came off the red stone called*carodé*. The strings on their sandals were also painted red.

ayoreo: Uje yocáquiode ore chatatapo uñeque rĩ uje chi ayoré dacasutedie gajnenie jé u. Chopise ayoré dacasutedie poti jnu ãri adode rĩ. Uje chi ipesudé ayoré dacasutedie nanique jnu ãri.
english: (Oidábiadé said: I would like to make this comment, our forefathers did not yet know long ago about the influence of Satan’s demons, or that Satan is the author of killing.)

ayoreo: *Cajoidé –  Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985.*
english: The medicine man has the power to curse as well as heal. When a medicine man mistreats or curses people and causes them to die, however, the people kill him for he has become a danger to his people.

ayoreo:
Chi Dupade
chojninga: “Beyoi yiroquéode bajaque.” Dupade iroquéode que etócho gotique ujetiga
yicoi ga yiquíjnongosi gánic. Dupade ojningane u ujeta yicoi pate omone to.
Pate omone u ujetiga yiyágo yoquisijnaringuéone ga yicoi Jesute, Dupade ore
acajnaquéode.
english: *Dupade*says to us: “First obey my commands.” It is not hard to obey*Dupade*, and it will never make us tired.*Dupade*is saying that we should be doing good things. It’s a good thing to stop sinning, and to start doing what Jesus, and our father*Dupade*, teach us to do.

ayoreo:
Jesus chojninga:
“Que iraja yiríringárique dire, mu a yiyague uacamánique,” chojninga.
english: Jesus said: “I don’t know when I’ll return, but suddenly I’ll be there!” That’s what he said.

ayoreo: Mu*Jupi*chojninga: “Be*Iyai*gari ga amóji.”
english: But I left*Orégajnúi*because he was jealous of*Jupi*, my former husband.

ayoreo:
Dupade imaningone unojo cucha
iji uruode u uje que taque Dupade gotique. Ũrepise Dupade. Dupade imani tu
tuté dire, jec u diri, dirode uje yimóngo iji dirode ujnojo uje Dupade pésu gu
jetiga ayoréode yimóngo. Yujnusinángo ipesudode cucha ga yirajágo uje Dupade
Uruode ũrepíso uje imóngo Dupade ipesudode cucha.
english: Dupade, it’s said, made these animals to have as their land the ground, so that the things he had finished making would all have their own environments.

ayoreo: Enga*Dupade**Uruode*uté u Jesute uje pésu cuchade jnese iji taningai; ga*Dupade**Uruode*jé jogate erámi bajac. Ga*Dupade**Uruode*jé u*Dupade*to. Erámi taningane u unojo uje*Dupade*pésu erámi, enga San Juan chingo yoque, chi: “Cuchade uje déji erámi jnese que ipesute re go.*Dupade**Uruode*ipesute cuchade jnese iji erámi. Jecute yingongo uac uje*Dupade**Uruode*ipesute erámi jogonórone cucha. Que cuchárique cuse ujetiga ca ipesute.*Dupade**Uruode*ipesute cuchade jnese.” *Dupade*chi utocaidi u Daruode (uje Jesute u) uje pésu cuchade. Enga*Dupade*chimate cuchade gu datocaidi iji erámi. Utocaidi u Jesute, dajnacari.*Dupade*chi pésu cuchade enga utocaidi u uje chimate cuchade. Ore chingo yoc uje pucuecaringuei garani tu uté to. Mu ijnangununie, pisijnaringuei pota sué gai mu que toc ujetiga sué*Dupade**Utocaidi*gai uje que sué*Dupade*gai gu. Que cucha uñeque sué*Dupade*gai uje*Dupade*ipesute cuchade jnese enga que cuchárique sué gai.
english: John tells us about when*Dupade*’s*Word*made himself into a man, just like us.*(John 1:14)* And*Dupade*’s*Word*was Jesus, the one who created all things so long ago.*Dupade*’s*Word*existed before earth was made. And*Dupade*’s*Word*was also*Dupade*. In the beginning*Dupade*created the earth, and the book of John tells us about that. He says: “The things that are in the world didn’t just make themselves.*Dupade*’s*Word*made everything in the world. Therefore, we are telling you that everything on earth was made by*Dupade*’s*Word*. There is nothing that he did not make.*Dupade*’s*Word*made it all.”

ayoreo: Ga ore doi ga ore chasẽra chugúpẽredatéi ga jiei ore ga ore chasẽra jnese. Ga chi chóna abuei. Chijna iyoi. Chi gajneque iyojna. Ga jé noñája abuei ajei. Gati ajei.
english: And when he stopped at the lake,*Diti*shot his arrows and killed him. He shot him and drug him off. And that is how he finished him off.

ayoreo: *Picanerane*ore erane u*Tarajane, Umarajane*ore ga ore tujua dáringane deyácho.
english: Two of the **Picanerãi Clan*tunes were **Tarajane*and**Umarajane*, and they would announce them when they sang.

ayoreo: Yijñecarósi iji de. Chiese*Ipeyai*nero yu nanic. Gajnei marínga yipógu yachachagúgode quitic ome*Uchiedie*ejoi.
english: *Tajerogue Chijñecare*

ayoreo: Ayoréode*yocadi*udé.*Yocadi*u cojñoque bajade adode gu. Mu yacaranguipísome don Len ore uje ore chise yoque, ga ore chingo Dupade uruode aja yoc uje que etoque gotique. Socase ti ga ore chajni yoque to ome dacajnaquéode. Etoc ore, uyoque. Que yotigáco yocacadisórone ore uruode. Yacaranguipis uje e jiei cojñone ore uje ore chingo Dupade Uruode aja yoque, ayoréode uyoque iji de Rincón.
english: And some of us*Ayor**eos*are like those first*cojñone*. But I’m very thankful that Len and his companions came to us, and taught us from*Dupade*’s*Word*, and they made it easy for us to understand. Hopefully they’ll return with more teaching. They are very strong and so are we. We don’t doubt the words of our teachers. I’m so grateful that there are so many people who are now teaching us the word of*Dupade*here in Rincón.

ayoreo: Ga arei ga yapúcoja jogadi uje yirajágo uje que yotique déji erámi ga yijóchago chicórone ga yimáco gu chicórone.
english: The “*Iyatade”*who prepare things for the Festival also paint their bodies black with carbon. They do it in different ways.

ayoreo: *Dijaide – Tobité, Bolivia – 1970**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Dijaide uruode udore*
english: We are thankful for this little teaching from Dupade’s words. This is all I have to say to us this afternoon. But maybe someone else is here who would like to share a little of Dupade’s words with us, and if so, feel free to do so.

ayoreo:
Que yetétique cuse go iji
Dupade. Yirasiguipise. Chamáñaje e yicatecã́ri gu.*(Santiago
4:8)*
english: There is nothing I don’t like about Dupade. I love him. That is because I now talk to him.

ayoreo:
Dupade, átaja yujodie chequei,
uyoque to gajneri, ga uneripise baruode yui ome yoque gajneri, yoque Dupade.
Yirajágo uje jiei yoque nanique uje yoco cucha bisideque iji baruode, mu que
yirajágo gotique gu, mu e yirajágo uje que cucha bisideque ua gotique, yoque
Dupade. Yiraja uje baqueta togode nanique iji taningane, yoque Dupade. Que
uñeque cuse gotique jeta chiqueta totique. Gusu ua uje baqueta togode nanique,
yoque Dupade.
english: Our Dupade, I thank you for helping us to grow in our understanding of your Word. Help us not to forget the things our teachers have taught us about you. Our Dupade, you said, “Whoever is ashamed of me, I will also be ashamed of that person when I return to earth.” This is what you said a long time ago when you were here on earth.

ayoreo:
Ujetiga gosi
urúo udore, enga uté gosi cho parac. Cho jetiga yoquijnose carubode déji Dupade
gari nanique uje yiyógo áyipiedie gáyode gu. Enga yocajnei pujuyaqueque déji
yoquiquei. Mu Jesus cho yoquisijnaringuéone oré ome dáringai chuque uje toji
curesei gai ome yoque, enga jé u uje Dupade chĩro yoquijnose carubode udore
iji dari.*(Romanos
3:25,26 ore)*
english: **on earth****:**

ayoreo: Chojningame dasangṍri: “Arángome cucha sijnángone jne iji yiguíjnai.”
english: It was hot and he made the house for shade. The other children didn’t know that very soon*Guébi*’s heavenly parents were coming to get him.*Guébi*didn’t want to play, but the other children really wanted him to play.

ayoreo: Ore chejna yocugúchade enga ore chijna*Usigáidatéa*ujúbei ore.*Jusédacóde*dóji ore gari ga chumuña. Mu chijíñome ga cho parac enga ore chijnapo rĩ, jocaratade.
english: We arrived back in camp. But when we looked around we saw that they had killed*Jecude*and he was laying over there. They had beheaded him. They had robbed all of our things. They hid my things that they took because they were afraid of our father.

ayoreo: Ore chutai ore ome cojñone jeta ore chujé.
english: They would ask the Totem Pole to give them*cojñone (white people)*to kill.

ayoreo:
Uje Dupade chi chojnirame ore
diríca: “Ca acho jne. Acho cuchade jnese iji yejnai tuné, mu ca acho poria
tuaté adie jne.”
english: For this is what God had said to them: “Don’t eat from it. You can eat everything else in my garden, but don’t eat the fruit from that tree.”

ayoreo:
**Uroi catecã́ri Dupade:**Udúco baruode iji domingo tuné, yoque Dupade. Que yimo jeta barasique ua uje badute baruode iji yoquibijnani uje yacaco yocujode banguresóño ore, yoque Dupade. Mu ajusipise baruode guio iji dejai udé to, yoque Dupade, iji baruode jogadi udé. A yudúco baruode iji gajneri, yoque Dupade. Cáma yudúco que mu, a yudúco jne, yoque Dupade, enga ajusi yoquio gajneri uje uduco pacajnaquéode.

Davidé que déji guidai
marínga, mu yamátago yoque ome baruode. Que yiyágo gotique, yoque Dupade, mu
que toc jeta uñeque chacate yogome baruode, yoque Dupade, uje Davidé e jno,
yoque Dupade, mu a yacate yocurasade jne. Mu aródiji sañeque, yoque Dupade, Davidé,
Jómone ore ujetiga ca cuchárique gajnarénguiji ore, yoque Dupade. Enga jeta
chacate yoque dirome iji domíngoi, Davidé. Bamo uje chiraja baruode. Chiraja
baruode quigode uje chingo yoc iji domingone jnese, yoque Dupade.

Enga átaja yoque gajneri iji
baguíjnai udé uje bangári baruode gajneri iji yibijnani, yoque Dupade á.
Ujetiga jec areque gajneri baruode ome ua, yoque Dupade. Enga barasique yoque
gajneri jeta adute baruode. Icatecã́ri ua ome babi Jesus i. Amẽ.

**Uroi chatata Faraón urigode quigode:**Yajirigode u ore eduguéjna cuchábi Faraón urigode que iji durasé udé. O yáyugu uje yajire bisideque. Mu Don Len chise yu que, mu que yiraja yajiritique quitígo. Que yiraja yajéningo jeta yacate uagome. Uje yajire Faraón urigode.*(Genesis 41:7-16)*

Uje eduguéjna cuchábi Faraón chi
mo nanique iji dejai. Mu urigode gajnárecho. Enga chi chĩ́cara ga aquirájna
ueradedie doi. Mu quigade ga aquirájna godóguedíe chi doi dojodie aquirájna
urojnai enga chi chujé. Enga chi tagu ga jogadi chona.

Enga eduguéjnai cho dáyugu re.
Uje urigode gajnárecho ome re. Mu chi mopo rĩ enga doi damoque ichai. Chi mo
mu urigode uñane chise. Maiz querúode chi doi Faraón urigode. Mu quigade ga
maiz cosocodábidie doi Faraón urigode. Ga maiz cosocodábidie chujé maiz
querúode. Enga tagu ga íjnoque jogadi. Mu eduguéjna cuchábi chi chojninga: “O
yáyugu yu ome yurigode uje gajnárecho ome yu.”

**Eduguéjnai tibidi daijna gajnaréngone:**Enga chojninga: “A yi daijna gajnaréngone ome yurigode jnenga ore chirajape jne. Uje gosi chiraja enga a yijnime yu iji yiyaridi tuné ga yicháji gosi taja.”

Enga chi doi daijna gajnaréngone
iji erámi. Chatatame ore mu que ore chiraja urigode go.

**Eduguéjnai tibidi José:**Mu quigade ga iroi uje chisi ome*vínoi*ome deduguéjnai narare enga chi chojninga: “U baroi uje bajuru gai uje i tu José. José u uje chiraja purigode to.”

Enga chi Faraón chojninga: “Be
enga chajire yurigode to. O yáyugu yu yurigode uje gajnárecho ome yu. Daijna
gajnaréngone que chajnerame yurírique gotique.

Chi ore doi José ga ore chijnája
eduguéjnai iguíjnai yui uje ore suru gai tiji. Chi ore chojninga: “José á,
uyoque u uje yibájoi ua. Jé ibájoi ua ujetiga araja eduguéjnai Faraón urigode.
Uje daijna gajnaréngone que ore chiraja go.”

Enga chi ore chécãre José
iguidedie. Chi ore doi oite ueradi ga ore chadíja. Tujúna dajúnone to. Chécãre
dadode ga ore chijnímiji eduguéjna iguíjnai uje ore suru gai tiji. Enga chi jno
ga chajnai Faraón yui.

**Eduguéjna cuchábi Faraón chatata darigode ome José:**Enga chi chise Faraón enga chi chojninga: “Faraón á, ¿Jé gotique?”

Mu chi Faraón chojninga: “José
á, qué icatecã́ri ua ujetiga arajape yurigode. Yurigode gajnárecho ome yu iji
dejai seque. Ujetiga arajape yurigode,” chojninga Faraón ome José.

José chi chojninga: “Iraja.”

Faraón chojninga: “José á,
yícãra enga cabayu urojnanie déji tié edúdi. Mu quigade ga cabayu godogábidie
doi aquira urojnanie ga chujé. Enga tagu ga íjnoque jogadi. Ga siete godogábidie
rĩ,” chi chojninga. “José á, ujnojo que, yurigode. Ga gajnárecho ome yu.”

Enga José chi chojninga: “Iraja.”

Mu Faraón chojninga: “Imopo que
rĩ iji yimoque ichárique enga yimo que mu maiz querúode doi yu iji yurigode.
Mu que yiraja gotique uje maiz cosocodábidie dopoi yu rĩ. Doi yu enga doi
maiz querúode. Enga chujé enga tagu enga a chona.”

**José catecã́ri Dupade ome Faraón urigode quitígo:**José e quejnáji Dupade yui jeta chátaja ome Faraón urigode quitígo.

José chojninga: “Faraón á,
Dupade irajatíga uné. Faraón, barigode udore chi imóringui sequeré noñangue
jne, siete sequeredie enga chi imóringo chaguhéo iji erámone jne,” chojninga
José ome Faraón. “Mu que yirajatíga go. Dupade irajatíga uje chingo yu,” José
chojningame.

Chi chojninga que, eduguéjna
cuchábi: “Que yirasique yu ome yurigode go. Daijna gajnaréngone que ore
chajnerame yurírique go ome yurigode jeta ore chiraja,” Faraón chi chojninga, “Mu
a yejna daijna gajnaréngone ore iji erámone,” chi Faraón chojninga. “Uje que
pirajaque yurigode go ome ore.”

Uje chojninga que: “José á, a
yijnime yu iji yijogadi te jne, ugúsi ucase enga a ore cho deduguéjna iji ua
jnese jne iji sequeré omenie, jé iji sequeré noñánguenie to.”

**Chi cojñone uñéngo chingo darigode aja José to uje qué déji ore jurúgaipídi ajei:**Enga chi ore chatata darigode ome José jnese.

Chi uñeque chojninga to: “José á,
arajape yurigode to,” chojninga. “Que yiraja gotique que uje yiji enga yiji
durasede enga, José, yiji durasede iji yurigode enga yijnoque yibosode iji
canásta iji yatoi durasede. Mu chugúpẽre chagúode iji gatajnoque tagu yibosode
iji yatoi durase,” chojningame José.

Mu José e chojninga: “Iraja. Enga
imóringo u uje ujetiga barósõri tuté irógode chujé ua jne enga ore tṍra ua
aja ajãro enga chugúpẽre chagúode tagu ua iji guesi.”

Quigade ga uñai chojninga: “José
á, yĩ́cara to iji dejai tuqué ga cojñotique chisi úvadie adie ome yu,”
chojninga, “Cojñotique chisi ome úvadie adie ome yu iji yurigode,” chojninga.

José chojninga: “Iraja. Yiraja
arĩa barigode. Udore imoi chi u ujetiga chiroque ua iji quigade. Bajne tu úva
adie ujetiga apésu eduguéjna cuchábi jotai vínoi ome udire. Enga uacarigode
quigode u unojo. Mu que yirajatíga go. Dupade irajatíga uje chingome uaque,”
José chojninga.

**Uroi chojninga: ‘Yajirápo aníri Juan, Pedro ore adode rĩ iji Hechos:**Juan a chojninga: “Pedro á, yico ga icoi Dupade iguíjnai jne uje guedé cho jñuque. Yicoi ga yicatecãcoi Dupade iji jne.”

Enga Juan, Pedro ore jno ga ore chicáji
Dupade iguíjna cuchábi. Chi ore chajire Dupade iguíjnai piagoi mu ore chimo jnani
iji uje noñaque. Noñai uté qué chirote cojñone ore iji Dupade iguíjnai piagoi.
Noñai u uje chi noñángaja guesi tiji daté. Datéringa uje a chugutana dagutadedie
aja Dupade iguíjnai piagoi. Ga chirote cojñone ore iji. Mu chi Juan, Pedro ore
tṍraji to ome Dupade iguíjnai. Mu ore chajire daquei tiji ga e ore chimo
noñai tiji. Enga qué chirote cojñone tiji Dupade iguíjnai piagoi.

Chi noñai iji chojninga: “¡Juã́! ¡Pedro á! ¡Uaque!” chi chojninga. “Yicatecã́ri uac
ujetiga asíyope yu iji cucha ajóningárique,” chi chojninga peroi. “Uacamóño uje
peroque yu ga que toque cucháringome yu.”

**Juan, Pedro ore chisiome cucha ueradi ome peroi uje chutai cucha ajóningárique ome ore:**Enga Juan, Pedro ore chojninga: “Iraja.” Chi ore chojningame peroi: “Iraja baruode, mu íjnoque yoquiplátadie. Íjnoque*oro*iji yoque.”

Mu Pedro chi chojningame peroi: “Peroi
chá, íjnoque yiparátadie, íjnoque cuchade iji yu, pijotade ujetiga yisi ome
ua. Mu a yisi ome cucha ueradi ome ua,” chojninga Pedro. “A yisi ome cucha
ueradi ome ua jne, ¡cucha ueradéjna!”

Mu peroi chi chojninga: “Juan,
Pedro, uacamóño yu to nanique uje ibasíji ité nanique ga noñaque yu aja guesi
nanique. Enga qué yugúsiji de iji dirode unojo. Ga asíyope yu iji uacabotique,
jeoga uacapláta ajóningárique. Uacamóño to uje peroque yu nanique iji ité ajei
ga jé qué u aja dirode udojo.”

**Juan, Pedro chiqueta peroi nanique uje ore chĩ́care Jesuse i:**Mu Juan, Pedro ore chojninga: “Peroi chá, yiraja mu íjnoque cuchade iji yoc, mu a yisígo ome cucha ueradi aja ua enga cucha ueradi tuté u Jesuse i.”

Chi Juan, Pedro ore chĩ́care
Jesus i enga ore chojninga: “Be guesáringai tuté ga ayágue. Mu ayágue gajnerí
enga asa bagutadé uaté ome bãrachúa te enga ca apúsaja ome bajmainie, bajmanecade
ore. A asa baqué tome bãrachua te enga jeta asa ome naque gajnerí, mu qué
ayágue.”

Ga chi peroi doi guesáringai te
ga chi cháji enga doi ore uruode. A Juan jmanecai chi jé jogadi tiji ome peroi.
Mu Juan chojninga: “Ayague, Peroi chá, ayágue. Tatoguei á, ayague.”

Ga tatóguei doi uruode ga chi
chague. Enga chisa dogué ome dãrachua. Enga chague jmame, enga uqué e dique. Enga
cho omone. Cho jetiga etóguiji daté ajei nanique.

Mu Pedro, Juan chi ore chojninga:
“Que yirajatiguei go. Dupade irajatíga, Dupade ujopié tu yoidé.”

Ore chiqueta peroi. Mu Juan,
Pedro que ore iquetaque go. Dupade ujopié tu uje déji ore ajéode. Ga ore oidé
tu Dupade Ujopié aja quedéjnane uje chiqueta peroi. Ga ore irasique re uje ore chiqueta
peroi. Mu que ore iquetaque go. Dupade Ujopié u. Dupade chingo dajopié taja
Juan, Pedro ore. Ga ore chijna Dupade ujopié aja quedéjnane. Ga Juan, Pedro ore
chiqueta peroi, tatoi. Enga etoque iji dirode udojo. Cho jetiga etógaja guesi
nanique iji daté ajei. Ga chi peroi irasique re uje Juan, Pedro ore chiqueta.
Mu que ore iquetaque go. Mu ore iquetaque uje ore chijna Dupade ujopié aja
quedéjnane.

Garósi
to uje yajire Dupade Uruode to iji guedé jnoringai.

*Uroi – Tobité, Bolivia – 1970**Marina Morarie churúsãre Uroi uruode nanique*
english:
**You will hear your****W****ord from our lips**

*A badute Baruode iji yoquibijnani Uroi*

**Uroi’s prayer:**

We’ve heard your*W**ord*this Sunday,*Yoqui-**Dupade*. I don’t know if it makes you happy to hear your word being preached from our lips, but take care of it tonight,*Yoqui-**Dupade*, in this meeting. We are about to hear your*W**ord*, but haven’t heard it yet, so watch over us as we hear it.

We are gathering to hear your word. Even though Ecarai is not here, we are not abandoning our meeting at all,*Yoqui-**Dupade*. And since there is no one else to teach us your*W**ord*, I will teach it.

Davidé (Ecarai) is on a trip,*Yoqui-**Dupade*. Watch over him wherever he is and watch over Jómone that nothing bad happens to them,*Yoqui-**Dupade*. And may Davidé teach us next Sunday. You know that he knows your*W**ord*. He knows what your*W**ord*means and tells us about it each Sunday,*Yoqui-**Dupade*. Help us today here in your house as you hear your word from our lips,*Yoqui-**Dupade*. May you approve of how it is taught. May it be taught correctly so that you do approve of it,*Yoqui-**Dupade*. And may you be happy with us as we hear your*W**ord*. I pray to you in the name of your Son Jesus. Amen.

**Uroi tells about****Pharaoh’s dream:**

I read this morning about King Pharaoh’s dream. Just reading it left me confused. Don Len came to see me, and I told him I didn’t understand what I had read. I didn’t know how it applied to us. This is what happened when I read about Pharaoh’s dream.

King Pharaoh was sleeping one night. But he had a bad dream. He dreamed about some healthy cattle. But afterwards, some sickly, skinny cows attacked the fat cattle and killed them. And they devoured them and nothing of them was left. And the king was troubled. The dream he had was very ugly.

He went to sleep again and while he was sleeping he had another dream. This one was about tall, healthy stalks of corn, and that is what Pharaoh’s dream was about. After this, another dream came to him and it was about spindly, poor stalks of corn. And the spindly, unhealthy corn killed the tall, healthy corn. And it devoured it and nothing of the good corn was left. But the king said: “I am troubled by my dreams because they were very ugly to me.”

**Pharaoh called for****‘daijnane’****to interpret his dream****s****:**

And he said: “I’m going to call in the*daijnane*and have them tell me what my dreams mean. If any of them can tell me what they mean I’m going to let them rule in my place.”

And so, he brought in all the*daijnane*in the land. He told them about his dreams, but they did not know what they meant.

**Pharaoh called for Joseph****to interpret his dreams****:**

But afterwards the servant who served the wine said: “There is one of your servants in prison named Joseph. He knows about dreams.”

And Pharaoh said: “Bring him and perhaps he can interpret my dreams. I’m still troubled about the bad dreams I had. Those*daijnane*couldn’t explain my dreams at all.”

So, they brought Joseph, and they took him to the king’s palace from where he was imprisoned. They said to him: “Joseph, we have come to you and are taking you to interpret King Pharaoh’s dreams. The*daijnane*could not interpret them.”

They changed Joseph’s clothing. And brought out a new, and beautiful robe for him to put on. He also was given a neckpiece to wear. He was totally changed from how he was in the prison. And he left and went toward Pharaoh’s palace.

**Pharaoh told his dreams to Joseph:**

And when Joseph reached Pharaoh he said: “King Pharaoh, what do you want?”

But Pharaoh said: “Joseph, I wanted to speak to you and ask you to interpret my dreams. Last night I had some bad dreams. Tell me what they mean.” This is what Pharaoh said to Joseph.

Joseph said: “I understand.”

But Pharaoh said: “Joseph, I dreamed about seven fat cattle on the banks of a river. Then, I dreamed of some unhealthy, skinny cows that went after the fat cattle and killed them and ate them and left nothing of them. There were seven of the skinny cows.” Then he said: “Joseph, these were my dreams. They were very bad dreams to me.”

And Joseph said: “I understand.”

But Pharaoh said: “I slept again and had another dream and I saw some corn plants in my dream that were tall and healthy. Then for some reason, I dreamed of corn that was straggly and unhealthy. They went after the tall, healthy plants and killed them and ate them and left nothing behind.”

**Joseph prayed to****Dupade****about the meaning of Pharaoh’s dreams:**

Joseph hurried to pray to*Dupade*that he would tell him the meaning of Pharaoh’s dreams.

Then, he said: “Pharaoh,*Dupade*knows all about this. The meaning of your dreams is about seven years of drought, when there will be famine all over the earth.” This is what he told Pharaoh. And then he said: “But I do not have such knowledge at all. It was*Dupade*’s knowledge and he revealed it to me. This is what Joseph told Pharaoh.

The great king said to him: “I was unhappy about my dreams. The*daijnane*could not explain them to me, because they didn’t know what they meant.” Then Pharaoh said: “I’m going to get rid of those*daijnane*all over the earth, because they couldn’t tell the meaning of my dreams.”

And he said to Joseph: “I’m going to remove myself from ruling from my throne and you will rule during the seven years of plenty, and the seven years of famine.”

**Before this, some other men had told Joseph their dreams:**

Before this, two others had told Joseph their dreams.

One of them said: “Joseph, interpret my dream, also.” That’s what he said to Joseph. “I don’t understand this but, in my dream, it was early morning. And, Joseph, in my dream I was carrying food in a basket on my head. But vultures from above came down and ate the food from the basket I was carrying on my head.” This is what he told Joseph.

But Joseph said to him: “I understand. The meaning of your dream is that your master’s servants are going to kill you, throw you into the village plaza and the vultures will eat you up.”

Then the other man said: “Joseph, I had a dream, too, in the night, and someone gave me fruit from the grape vines. A*cojñoi*gave me some grapes in my dream.” This is what he said.

Joseph said: “I understand. I know the meaning of your dream. It means that your master will let you serve him again, and you will bring him wine to drink. But I didn’t know what your dream meant, it was*Dupade*’s knowledge that he gave to me. That is how I knew the meaning of your dream.” This is what Joseph told him.

**Uroi****shares****from the book of A****cts****about****what John and****Peter****did.**

Probably John said: “Peter, let’s go to*Dupade**’s big house*(the temple) when the*sun makes its arc*. Let’s go for the hour of prayer, and we will pray to*Dupade**.*”

So, John and Peter left to go to*Dupade**’s big house*. It’s said they looked at the door of*Dupade**’s big house*, and they saw a crippled man sitting there. The lame man was waiting for the people to go through the door into the temple. He had been crippled since birth. And the only way he could stand was by leaning on walking sticks. And he was waiting for the*cojñone*at the door of*Dupade**’s big**house,*so they would give him things.

And so it was that John and Peter came to where he was at the door of temple. And arriving, they saw the lame man. And he was waiting for people who were going into the temple.

The lame man said: “John! Peter! Both of you! I am asking you to give me something for my livelihood.” This is what the crippled man said to them. “You see, I am crippled and can not work for a living.”

**John and Peter gave****the lame man****something beautiful:**

John and Peter said to him: “We understand.” Then they said to the lame man: “We understand what you are saying, but we have no money, neither have we any gold.”

But Peter said to the lame man: “Lame one! I have neither goods nor money to give to you. But I’m going to give you something much more beautiful.” Then he said: “What I am going to give you is not just beautiful, it’s very, very beautiful.”

But the lame man said: “John! Peter! You know that I’ve been crippled since I was born from my mother long ago. That’s why I’m here every day. So just give me some of your food, or a little of your money for my sustenance. You know I was crippled in my mother’s womb, and I have been unable to walk ever since, to this very day.”

**John and Peter healed the lame man by mentioning the name of Jesus:**

But John and Peter said: “Lame one! I know how it is with you, but I have nothing to give you, but there is something beautiful for you and that beautiful thing is the name of Jesus.”

Then John and Peter spoke the name of Jesus and they said: “Go outside and stand up. Stand up and lean on your walking stick. Take it in your right hand and don’t depend on your hands and arms. Just grab hold of it with your right hand and rise up.”

The lame man went outside and did what they said and rose up. Maybe John held out his arm to help him. But John said: “Stand up, Lame one, stand up. Crippled one, stand up.”

And the crippled man did what they asked and stood up. And he probably held onto his cane with his right hand. And he stood right up, and he walked! He walked as normally as anyone. It was as though his limbs were strong inside his mother’s womb.

But Peter and John said: “This was not done by our ability to heal. The ability of*Dupade*’s son Jesus did this. What is what we used,*Dupade*’s ability and power.”

They healed the lame man. But they were not really the ones that healed him. He was healed by*Dupade*’s power inside them. Wherever they went*Dupade*’s power went with them.*Dupade*showed John and Peter his power. And they carried that power with them everywhere. And that is how Peter and John healed the lame man. And from that time on he was strong. It was as though he’d been born strong from the time his mother gave him birth. The man who had been lame was very happy to be healed by Peter and John. But he wasn’t healed by them at all, but by the power of God that they carried with them wherever they went.*(Matthew 19:26)*

These few things are what I read in*Dupade*’s*Word*this afternoon.

Yoqui-Dupade – Our God

Dupade’s Word – God’s Word

Dupade – God

Dupade’s big house – Temple

The sun makes its arc – 2 p.m.

Daijnane – Magicians, witch doctors, shamans

Cojñoi – Person who is not of the Ayoré tribe

*Uroi –**Tobité, Bolivia**– 1960.*

*Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Jé yirasiguipise yájnatigo ore ome carataque-chagúode-daté uaqué.
english: I am so happy about what my grandsons did to that very fierce, man-eating jaguar.

ayoreo: *(1 Corintios 14:40)*Uje*Dupade*chi chojningame Moises uje chi chiqueta raja, chi chojninga: “Jeque acáji poria uaté jne uje pioi tṍraji, mu ejñúape barídade bajaque, ga jé u ujeta abágui yu,” chojninga.
english: You may be thinking that I am scolding you, but that is not what I am doing. In another place we are told to exhort each other. And this is my exhortation – I am reminding you of what you already know – that the one we now listen to is*Dupade*, just as we once listened to the little bird. We no longer consider Asojna the one to worship and serve, now we serve the living*Dupade*and do the things he says to do. We used to fear the little bird and that is why we obeyed her, but when we heard that it is*Dupade*who truly loves us, we responded to his great love and now we obey Him. I am reminding you of how our forefathers once listened carefully to the little bird and did all that was required of them, not ignoring any of the things she asked them to do. And that’s is how we should be with*Dupade*.

ayoreo: “Angoño yu ga yijína ore que á,” yojninga. Ga ore chacáji ta. Mu cojñone iji ta ga ore chojninga, “¡Boyo!”
english: “Tell me what to do and I’ll go with them,” I said. And they entered the jungle. But the non-Ayoré person said: “Run!”

ayoreo:
*Dupade*,
adute yoquicatecãraitigade unojo,*Dupade*.
Jecucha icatecãcoi ua,*Dupade*, ome
guidaigosode ore,*Dupade*, uje yocujo
ayoré ore gu. Aqueta ore to,*Dupade*,
iji pisijnaringuéone,*Dupade*. Jec adi
u ujetiga ajna uñéngo to ome baruode,*Dupade*,
ujetiga uñéngo changureta babi Jesuse to,*Dupade*.
english: *Dupade*, hear these prayers of ours. We have been praying for the*Guidaigosode*for a long time, for they are our fellow Ayoréos. Save them, also,*Dupade*, from sin. In that way you can take some of them and, through your Words, bring them to faith in your*Son Jesus*.

ayoreo: Pésu dejnai. Que yimo jeta pésu dejnatique, mu bamo uje ayoréode uyoque yocucuecaringuei garani tu yoquejnane. Chise gójni Domíngonie enga a pésu Dupade Uruode ome darásade. Enga a uaque gosi a doi Dupade Uruode unojo enga a uaque gosi sue Dupade Uruode jnungórone gosi gai.
english: Sometimes*Dupade*’s servants depend on*Dupade*to supply their needs. Others depend on the work of their own hands for their livelihood. Some live by faith, depending on their preaching of*Dupade’s Words.*This type of person surpasses us –Ayoreos, and Cojñone all of us in this world.

ayoreo: *(Juan 3:16-18)*Mu ome Dupade anguretasórone uje Dupade uruode chingo yóguiji daruode, darujna arone, enga chojninga: Gosi uje changureta Dupade, a ca chimopo, anínique, pujuyaquériguiji Dupade idai jne rĩ. A ca chimopo chaguhénique gotique rĩ. A ca chimo cucha ãréraque iji Dupade idai uté jne.
english: So, you can see that a person might not have killed anyone, or done anything else wrong, but still may not have truly believed in*Dupade*. And so,*Dupade*will not take him to heaven. The rich young man was really enjoying being rich. So he didn’t really understand*Dupade*’s Word. But when the day would finally come, and he would find himself in*the lake of fire*, only then would he regret holding on to his things, remembering in vain what it was like to be rich. He would recall how plentiful his food was on earth and how he never got hungry, but never again would he have a sense of well-being in this place of an unending second death.

ayoreo: “*Cucha Urõsódatéi*á, bamataté yájnatigo, yatígo ore ome cucha dacadode yúode uje yirãrare ome*Dosadídacóde*iji yejode. Ga yojningame*Dosadídacóde*iji yejode: ‘Jé cachi que ducunire maringa, jé ca yo yinamáji jnacarode ore iji yejode, yujnacarode, yatígo ore iji yirosadode udore.’
english: *Oh, you who caused such awful harm in the past*, you who tried to belittle my grandsons and my sons by threatening their futures. I spoke to*Dosadi’s*grandmother by my side. I said to*Dosadi’s*grandmother by my side: ‘Even though we aren’t*cojñone (*white people), I consider the young men about me as strong as ‘brush fortresses,’ my young sons and older sons for they are all around me, protecting me.’

ayoreo: Darujna – ga ore churúsãre gai; ore chicha cucha pibosode iji údi.
english: For the Totem Pole they used the tall cactus called*Darujna*– they wrote on it; they put food before it.

ayoreo:
A yujóraque ayoré narari jóroque
gajneri: ‘Gayujna todo uati ga que chajnai urúrique go.’
english: I want for*Dijaide*to build a house where we could live alone. Then I wouldn’t have to listen to all the things the women say. They tend to gossip most about those of us women who are believers.

ayoreo: Ujnacari urẽjai chise ta ore idai ujaminóri. E chucué to dacotei mu e chijno uje chirota dacotei. Ga chijno poridie jeta dajei uje ore chaquesu, chaquesu uje ore chucué cuchade.
english: Their mother said: “Hurry up. We must look for your father. Surely that big killer*Dachagaidé*has killed him.”

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: And that’s when the wild pigs started running away from the Ayoreos. They still run from us, because they know what we’re like. And that’s because of what the Ayoré that they took away so long ago told them. Wild pigs know our ways, and that is why, knowing all about us today, they still run away from us.

ayoreo: Enga yajni ga ore chijíñome yu uje yajníngo. Yajníngo. Ducosi tagu yóguiji ta ga yitoi. A yo sẽre mu a yiquecapo rĩ. Enga yicṍraja ta. Ga yijosa yedodie aja jnumi. Yĩriji enga yóji yiyojnai iji ti.
english: I got a fever and I passed out. I probably fainted, because when I came to I was flat on my face on the ground laying in my own blood.

ayoreo:
Yoque Dupade, yacaranguipis ome
ua uje e baqueta Teresa, yoque Dupade. Bamo uje ejnareté dirica, mu baqueta,
yoque Dupade. Que uñeque cuse ujetiga chiqueta ejnaretátique; gusu ua uje
baqueta yoque. Yacaranguipis ome ua uje udúco baruode iji dirode jnese,
jnanione uyoque.
english: Dupade, help my fellow women and myself to love your Word, our Dupade. We know that there are many of us who do not pay attention to your Word, but I do not know why, because I know that your Word is not something unimportant. Instead, it is powerful. When you were on earth, you even raised the dead with your Word, our Dupade. There is no other person who has raised a dead person, only you can do this.

ayoreo:
Mu chojninga:
“Acatecã́ri Yapade ome, jeta araja Yapade,” chojninga. Enga icatecã́ri enga ojninga iji dejai tiji yurigode: “Dupade é, qué icatecã́ri ua iji ne. Yacaranguipise uje babi Jesus chiqueta raja yu, enga catecã́ri yu ome, enga qué icatecã́ri ua ome yu to uje urõsoringuéone uásejna iji yu ujeta átajabá aja yu to,” chiese yojningame Dupade iji yurigode. “Átajape yejnaréngone aja yu,” ojninga iji yurigode ome Dupade. “Yacaranguipise uje babi Jesus chiqueta raja yu,” ojninga.
english: I said: “I know who you are. Who I think you are, dear one, is Jesus, although I’ve never seen you.”

ayoreo:
Mu jnacari chi jnacare bajai chi
chojnirame Dupade: “Ugúsi ti que.”
english: But the young man, the first man, said to God: “I was over there.”

ayoreo: *Corabé*jorã tu*Guiriamia*ga ore cho dajõra ome ñane. Ore chojningame ñane: “Yijõra.” Boquere ore ga ore canare dajmáinone jnacare. Chi ore ajmáini u*Caric Icha*, jnacare gatoi. Ore chuju datui aja guesi. Ore chajningarase gatererodie. Que sueapo rĩ. Ore canare gu. Ujo gapudie ore chojningame*Corabé*: “Aya bajmáinone. A topie baórique jne. Ore tujua pioi gueguerade.”
english: **Corabé (*White Butterfly) was friends with*Guiriámia*(type of bird) and they considered themselves best friends. They said to each other, “Friend.” They flirted and played around with the young men who liked them. It’s said that the one that really liked them was Caric Ichai, a very young guy. The girls would cut his hair and pull out his facial hair. It didn’t grow back, because they were always pulling it out, that’s why.

ayoreo: Corabé chojninga: “¡Aguito!” Chi e chisa gapua jmanai. Chi e chimánaja dari. Chi ore tagu uñane uredie iji gaté uje chimate raja.
english: Some would say,*“¡Pu á!*grab her!*¡Pu á!*grab her!” There were lots of people underneath the slippery tree. But they told them to clear out from underneath the tree to make room for the climbers.

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Manuélné atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed by: Joyce Davis Buchegger.*

ayoreo: Chi chojníngasia: “Yijína ua jne,” mu gajnárec uruábode. Iyajmánguiji jnumi gu, mu jé irasique re to. Chipocásusu dacarámi aja ñane ga que ore chirajási gotique.  Ore jose cucha bisidéguiji guei. Chisási dabade uje chirõre ore irengúi. Chidota re ome ore gu, ayoréode uje piogode déji. Dotãre ore irengúi gu. Areredatési*Guébi*ga que tõríasi piotique gu. Cási udui; déji acãrai, mu pósi. Chose yui. Dotãre ore irengúi gu uje piotígo déji. Mu isangóri chijnórasipísa. Chisasi jmanecábi.
english: He saw him asleep in the shadows of the trees. There was no doubt at all in his mind that he must have fallen there sometime back, and now he was resting on his little elbows on the ground. Having located the boy, the man knelt beside him and spoke to him. “Be my son, and go with me wherever I go,” he said.

ayoreo: ¡Mu cho jeta yirajapise Dupade Uruode to iji darujna aone! Mu a yacate uaque garósirique jne.
english: **A****yoré****people****all over the world** *Yicatecã́ri ome ayoréode iji erámi jnese Ipeyai*

ayoreo:
E yatata diríca iji ucacasé
semána bajei casé uje udute uacajnomei diríca iji de guidai uje chi uacatatayo
uaca-testimónio, ga yirasique uacaruode.
english: I don’t know if any of you, my companions, feel like I do about cigarettes – but I hate them. I’m afraid of people when they get drunk, also. I don’t like them to get near me.

ayoreo: “*Cucha Urõsódatéi*á, jé badi írique durasede uje que asururase paicarupe tiẽchui yirabai uqué durasede, ga qué yugúsiji yebiédatérigui. Ga uñeque datéraque qué yugúcoji yoquebiédatérigui iji uñeque quigáningo.
english: *You who caused such awful harm in the past*, why didn’t you react more quickly, then you could have thrown my grandson down, the one who has the beautiful line-markings as one of his **edopasade*(clan possessions). Then you would have seen me slobbering (Note: another way to refer to crying/lamenting) right now, along with the mother of another victim. We would both be lamenting the absence of our loved ones if you had been quicker – they would have become your victims, instead.

ayoreo: Jé u uje chi datũra changuñéra iyoi aja dabai ga noñáji aoi.
english: And then he said: “Grandmother, I killed*Natingarai*.”

ayoreo: *Ecarai – Campamento Ayoré, Bolivia – 1971**Faith Wyma churúsãre Ecarai atatagode udojo nanique*
english: Two Ayoré men could be hunting honey in the woods when Jesus comes back again; one of them has faith in*Dupade*, but the other one doesn’t. One would go up into heaven, and the other who is left behind will wonder what happened to his friend. He couldn’t accompany his friend to heaven, because he hadn’t put his faith in*Dupade*. But the other one did believe, and he went to heaven.  We Ayoré, here on earth, stick together -we go on trips together and go hunting, and hang out together, but afterwards we all return to camp. But when*Dupade*comes to take his believers, he might find us on a hunting trip together in the woods and take only the person that believed in Him. And that is why*Dupade*talks about how all of us on earth should believe in Him today.

ayoreo: Yequésãre. Yipesapo ore dai ejo rĩ, ore dai uñeque, mu tágape yuruode.
english: I’m going to curse someone and make another father for them. But I’m not telling the truth.

ayoreo:
Quigade ga Dupade chi pésu dírecaringuéi
iji taningai te ga pésu ijnangununie. Dejai ujetiga jé cãra re ome diri ujetiga
yimóngo iji, enga yoco yocujñare iji. Mu Dupade Uruode chatata uje chi gusu
yodode icaite iji jnumi iji taningai.
english: Dupade made this earth in the beginning, and in six days he completed everything. In the beginning, it says, Dupade saw that this earth was completely dark. So Dupade divided the darkness and pushed it together. And in the part where it had been daylight appeared. Dupade made everything so that we humans would not wonder how the earth was made. Dupade made daylight so that humans could work and travel around and serve Him. On the other hand, he made the night so that we people could rest and sleep.

ayoreo: Jecute Pablo chojninga: “Mu uje e uacarajáyo Dupade iji ejode, ga adi jé u uje Dupade chiraja uaque uje chimo uaque. ¿Jé gotique gajnerĩ a? ujetiga ajníñoja uacate bajade. Ore ate bajade u udore uje ore chetaque Dupade iji taningane nanique. Enga ore chetaque Dupade adode. Ga ore gajnui tu uje que ore chiraja cuchárique.
english: Therefore Paul said: “But afterwards, when you came to know*Dupade**,*then the Lord also knew you and looked after you. So, why have you turned back to the way you were in the beginning? Don’t you remember how people are who hate*Dupade?*They hate*Dupade,*and they hate his ways. They are like ignorant people who don’t know anything.

ayoreo:
Chi uné San Pablo chojninga: “¿Jé yipesutique ome ua gajnerĩ a?” chojninga. Mu Jesuse chojninga: “Bo ga
acáji oridaidatei, yingo bapesutígaja ua gajnerĩ.
english: That’s how it is with us, too, us*after children*. For Jesus changes our names, too, and we’re called Christians. True believers in*Dupade*are all called Christians. If you want to, you can also believe in Jesus. The way to Jesus is open to you. Nothing stands in your way. Jesus said, “Come unto me, everyone that wants to. The door is always open to my house, but it is your door that is closed to me.”

ayoreo:
Yojninga: “Señora Marina, jé
areque, yicatecã́ri jne.” Ga e yicatecã́ri.
english: I said: “Señora Marina, that’s right, I’ll talk to him.” And I now pray to Dupade.

ayoreo:
Unejaque
Davidé acaditigade, Yoque Dupade, enga yacaranguipis. Ajé bajmanai ujopié aja
yoquibai jnese, Dupade, yocurasade yóguiji ne gajnerĩ, Dupade. Enga
yacaranguipise, yoque Dupade, uje unéraja yoque Davidé acajnaquéode iji guedé
jnoringai tuné. Yacaranguipise ome baruode, Dupade. Yacaranguipise uje yingome
ua ome ore ji guedé jnoringai gajnerĩ.
english: *Davide’s*teachings are different,*Yoque Dupade,*and we are very thankful for that. Place your powerful hand upon all of us, all of those here in our village right now. Thank you,*Yoque Dupade,*for*Davide’s*good teachings this afternoon. And I’m thankful for your Word,*Dupade*. It’s a privilege for me to hold you up before those gathered here this afternoon.

ayoreo: Jĩrique yiyapo*Iyai*rĩ. Jĩrique yisapo yujode Etacõre*Jupi*rĩ. *Ijnamene – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: But*Jupi*said: “Go to*Iyai*and sleep with him.” I did, but quickly left*Iyai*also. And I went back to my fellow Étacõre clan member*Jupi*again.

ayoreo: Yocacaranguipise ome Dupade gajiné iji deréjnai tudé uje que yicoi to gotique udojo. Que yejnaréjo yoque go. Cho jeta Dupade chãraque yoque ome cucha gajnaréngone yúode gu yocúringai ujñepetaque. Mu que qui tu go. Dupade uruode déji to uje chi sijnaque yoque gu. Idájapíse yóguiji Dupade. Idája yoque jnese mu Dupade gajneque yoque. Dupa Pujnuéngaisṍri gajneque yoque jnese, mu yipójo yocadode ome uté, enga que cucha ome yoque uje Dupade chijnóra yoque. Yojnico uje yanguretágo Dupade, mu yicagóji quedéjnai ome iji ujade. (Uyu jé u to. Chamáñaje sijnaque cuchape yu gu.)
english: “People of this world are going to suffer,” Jesus said. “What we teach will be convicting to them and they won’t like us for it, but be strong. And remember how they hated the things I taught, as well.” Jesus Christ said: “And remember that I didn’t do anything wrong, but still they mistreated me. How much more will they mistreat you in the future.” Jesus Christ said: “But whoever goes through this with faith in Me, that one will be saved.”

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Jonanine, Acui.
english: *Carábiagosode***–**“People of the ‘carabia’.”

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Tajerogué iradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Tajerogue – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*

ayoreo: Dupade chojninga: “Ayoréode uaque, que uacáyipie tu yáyipie go. Que uacáyipie yáyipie go. Uacáyipiedie catadape. Jé yáyipie chise jnumi eruéode,” chojninga Dupade ome yoque. Jecute ujopierãque ga áyipie sué yoquibai.*(Isaías 55:8)*
english: God says: “People, your thoughts are not my thoughts at all. Not my thoughts at all! Your thoughts are ‘short,’ but mine fill the whole world.”

ayoreo:
Chi yocajneque
Jesute ojningane udore cucha gajnerĩ. Jesute ojningane cucha u uje chi ca
yimóngopo chaguhérique, ãréraque ome yoque gajnerĩ. Jecute Jesute chingo yoque
unojo aja yoque jeta yanguretágo Jesute ga yiyágo cucha gajnaréngone,
pisijnaringuéone, poriquéode, jé aja cucha gajnaréngone jnese, uje Dupade
chetaque, ga yicoi pate arégode to iji gajnerĩ.
english: The things that Jesus taught are for us today. What He tells us is that we won’t be hungry, and we won’t be thirsty anymore. The reason He tells us this is so that we might trust him and stop doing ugly things like stealing or anything else that*Dupade*hates, and instead, doing all the good that we can do.

ayoreo: Ore cho paraque, uje que ore chiraja daru quitígome yu. Mu quigade enga ore chojninga: “Yimóngo adode ga yirajágo uje emi cuse.”
english: I said to them, “That is right, and we also see what God has done. Look at the trees, the tall trees.  The fact that you live and exist is also evidence of the God who created you.”

ayoreo: Ore erásõre udé corã́cha jnungore adode ga u uje ore cho derásõri iji Chiquenone ore edópasa uqué ga ore edópasa queséode udore iji dati, jé aja guebéode. Ajiinerenie ore. Chiquenone edópasa guebéode udore; ome yocáquiode, ore edópasade jiei. Ore edópasa piguirané. Uje ayoréode ore chimochere gai nanique ga ore edópasa. Chequé bajedie nanique ore chojninga: piguirané. Ore edópasa guebéode rĩ udore iji dati. Ajinerenie jé aja guebéode udore jnese. Picanerane uyoque ga yoquedópasa udaji uacaguíjnane udáji, cochocoidie udaji; cochocoidie udiji ójo. Picanerane ore edópasa udiji ome yocáquiode ore. Ore chojninga: “Picanerane edópasa cochocoidie.” Uje chi Picanerane ore imané ójo nanique ga chi ore edópasa ójo.
english: They say: ‘I’m going to be like the armadillo and carry weapons.’

ayoreo: Enga*Guiéjnané*pojna*Ajópedái*. Chi ore chujérase mu que ore chujé go. Chi ore pojna ñane bisidec.
english: *Ajópedái*was offended and he began defending his sisters-in-law. He scolded*Guiejnanai.*

ayoreo: *Manuelné – Tobité, Bolivia -1960*
english: *Manuelné – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965*

ayoreo:
Mu Jesus
chojninga: “Yiraja. Acatecã́ri Yapade enga atata ore íode rĩ ome Yapade, enga
yiraja ore íode,” chiese chojninga.
english: This is what*Ichajúidé*seemed to say to him.

ayoreo: Jé garedie chajni ore gai iji degúi. Ga yico ga yicagoi tõõ. Yiquejnángome jocaratade ga yicagóji ¡tĩĩĩĩ! Mu*Ajópedé*chaca que. Tṍraji te ga yṍrangopísiji. Yijoco jocaratade aja. Jé*Ajópedé*a u. Mu yṍrangopísiji. Ga ore chaquĩ́ra. Ore chojninga,*Ajópedái*chojninga: “A yi ore iji degúi jne. A yingome*Guiéjnané*ore jne.” Uje pojna*Garáijnai*ore diríca.*Garáijnai*aja*Tocoidé*ore. Pojna ore údode diríca. A yibágui ore. Enga a yingome ore jne uje ore dorase yu ga ore chujérase yu. Mu a yipatíora yitique jne.*(*Mi víctima y yo, perezeremos juntos.*)”*
english: Only the children were in the camp. And we kept getting*doridie fruit*and then we heard people calling to our grandpa. And they thought we were there. They kept calling loudly to our grandpa. We were off getting*doridie*. We slept out there. We heard their calls and ran away. We were running and then*Dujuráidacóde*turned toward them. We returned to the fighters in the camp.  **Ajópedatéa*and them were there. But we kept running, all of us.*Ijnerainatéa*, who was an old lady at the time, came with us with*Ojo,*our little sister. And we kept running from the warriors.*Jésedaté, Sigaidaté*were with us.

ayoreo: Uyatágode que ore taguiji erámi. Yoquitódi u jeta ayorérique cho paraque ga chipese dotique cutepec ga chujé ayorérique.
english: The participants don’t eat when entering the jungle. What we fear is that someone will make a mistake and start sucking honey and it will kill someone.

ayoreo:
**A jé adúcho to yatatagode uje Jnaníne a e chatata:**Yirasique uje uacatijáyome*Dupade Uruode*iji dé. A yise idai uñane uje que ore doi*Dupade Uruode*to iji ta guidai, enga a yingo uacajnomei ome ore dirome.

**Balaam, gachidi burro ore:***(Numeros 22:21-34)*Mu yingo uaque garósi uje chi anire, Balaam gachidé burro tocade ome dachisṍri nanique. Gachisṍri ájningarángome dachidi nanique. Mu gachidi chimo*Dupade*irótique gate unirísõre iji daquei enga chicai quedéjnai ome. Mu gachisṍri que chimo cuchárique uje áyipie déji dagaidode cucha. A jé uacadúcho to udojo*Dupade Uruode*garósiode uje ore chacate uagome gu.

**Yacajnaquéode u udojo to ome yurásarigo iji Tobité:**Yingome ore garósiode to ica,*Dupade Uruode*iji ta, mu que ore changureta yu to gotique. Ore chotigase. Ga jnanione uñane chojninga: “Chamo jóroque gotique uje ore chácari Tobité iji yiquei uté. Que cuchárique tṍraji yiquéode,” ore chojninga.

Mu yojningame ore: “Anguretáyo*Dupade*ga yanguretágo jnese. Uacamóño uje chi*Dupade*diapo jne rĩ,” yojninga.

Yingome ore aníri, uje chi “*Dupade*pésu jnumi iji taningai nanique
enga jé arajáyo to iji uacacadisórone ore pijnanone,” yojninga.

Mu chi yotigayasórone garipise.
Yotigayasórone jõra Ayoi. Ga Ichorai.

Yojninga: “Yipesupu ga ca uacuñeque
chejnárengapo iji ejo rĩ,” yojninga. Yapúsaja yuruode ome ore. Mu quigade ga
Betidé dosirase Ayoi. A jé arajáyo to.

Mu yicatecãrápoi*Dupade*iji ore gari rĩ. Yojninga: “Átaja uté gajnerĩ ujeta bajéo ga ecapo gajnerĩ, uje chotigasipise baruode.”

Mu eca. Mu quigade diríca ga yingo garósi*Dupade Uruode*aja ore mu chojninga: “A jec ũré*Dupade*cuse jõ. Uje cuchárique chujerase yu,” chojninga, “Ga yitecurásu.” Tecu iji jnumone.

**Jnani ditécai, Juan, Pedro ore:***(Hechos 3:1 aja 8)*Ga isocai u Juan, Pedro ore uje chi ditécai doi ore uruode nanique enga dique. A jé arajáyo to.

Juan, Pedro chi ore jno. Ore doi*Dupade Uruode*iji iglésia. A jé
arajáyo to. Mu chi ore chise jnani ditécai. Jé uacamóño to jnani ditécai. Chi
jé tecurasu. Chi ore chise enga chojninga: “¿Acayóji rique a?” Enga chi chutai
ore. Chi chojninga: “Asíome uacaparátarigui ome yu jne.”

Mu Pedro chojninga: “Cho jeta yiparátaraque gotique jõ.”

“Mu que uacaparátaraque cuse ga asíome yu uacabotígo ome yu,” chojninga.

Udore uje yingo uaque garósi, garósi. Jé umári yacajnaquéode ica mu a garósi jé yacajnaquéode iji de ome uaque. Yicatecã́ri*Dupade*ga, ome uqué uje tecurasu, Betidé, uje uacarajáyo to, Ayoi.

Mu Josédé e areque to iji ujade. E doi*Dupade Uruode*to. E irasique re iji ujade to rĩ.

Chi ore chojninga ucode, Juan, Pedro ore: “Yisi ome cucha ueradéjnai ome ua jne.” Jé uacamóño to uje jnani ditécai isocai u *Matéo iji guidai. Mu jé tupo rĩ. Chi ore chojninga: “Yisígome yocugúcha ueradéjnai ome ua gajnerĩ. Ga cucha ueradéjnai uté u*Jesucrísto i.*”

Enga ore ojningai u Jesus ujopié
uje tẽrája ore ujetiga ore chiqueta dojode ayoréo. Ga ore chisa jmanecai ga
chague uqué. A jé adúcho to jeta ore chacate uagome.

Garósi yacajnaquérigo mu yacaranguipísome*Dupade*uje a chátaja yoque gajnerĩ. Udore uje ingome uaque garósiode.

**Yingo yacajnaquéode udore ome ore ica rĩ iji uta Pailón:**Yingo ore ica rĩ iji uta Pailón mu Dugúidé chojninga: “A jecucha dadi jorónga ome ayoréode yocuñeque – chirajapise uruode to jóroque ja,” chojninga.

Mu uacajnuéño*queyibáyode*iji de Rincón. Que yicoi*Dupade Uruode*to go iji Tobité. Yicoi to, yacaco ore, mu gadiógosi ore iji ajei. Jé uaque ga jiei uaque. Gagájnisi ore, cinco su ore iji ujade. Jecucha ga yingome ore mu que ore doi to go. Ore chotigase.

Mu yimeseranie gare iji chequedie
ore iji guidai. Yimesera uaté anire, jé uacarajáyo to uje ore chojningame
nanique Timingue. Yimeserapise to ga yimesera Eruidaté to rĩ. Yimesera uaté to
– Dapuré uje dopísi to*Dupade Uruode*to. Catecã́ri ome uáguiji de gatuánguiji deréjnane to iji durasedie, dejade
ore.

**E gusu yacajnaquéode ome uaque:**Garósi. Jiei yacajnaquéode to iji ujade mu e gusu yacajnaquéode. Cachi iyajmaque yu iji gu.

Mu jecucha ajnusínaño*Jesucrísto i*, uje ueradéjnapise gu. Pujopié déji i to, ga jé cho Pablo urusarane.*(Filipenses 2:9-11)*

**Mateo u Ecarai,
Tomása ore abi uje pero iji dabatigai nanique enga que dique to gotique.*

*Ipeyai – Rincón de Tigre, Bolivia – 1970**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Ipeyai acajnaquéode*
english:
**Thrilled with****Dupade****’s****Word****s**

*Uacatijáyome Dupade Uruode iji dé*

**You have probably heard****this teaching****from Jnanín****é****.**

I’m happy that here in Rincón you are thrilled with*Dupade*’s*W**ord**s*. When I go to other villages, in places where they are not meeting for*Dupade*’s*Word**s*, I’m going to tell them about you.

**Balaam and his donkey:***(Numbers 22:21-3**4**)*

I’ll tell you a little about a donkey that burst out and talked to his owner. But in the beginning the donkey’s owner doubted his donkey and was angry with him. But the donkey could see*Dupade*’s angel in front of them and went in another direction. But the owner couldn’t see the angel. His mind was on the things he had to do. You’ve probably heard something about this when they’ve taught you.

**I also taught the people in Tobité about****this story****:**

I told them a little bit about Balaam and his donkey, but they wouldn’t believe it. They doubted my words. Some of the men said: “Why in the world would they get up before me here in Tobité and teach such a thing. Nothing would come from such a teaching like this.”

But I told them: “Have faith in*Dupade*and then we will all believe. You know that*Dupade*is going to come back again.”

I told them they should believe*Dupade*’s*Word**s*because in the beginning*Dupade*made the earth, just as they’d heard from the mouths of their other teachers.

There were two who were most vocal in doubting me. One of them was Ayoi. The other was Ichorai.

I said, “May neither of you suffer for your unbelief later on.”

I said other things to them. But afterward they attacked Bétidé whose other name is Ayoi. You probably know all about this.

Then I prayed with them again.  I said, “Help him, if it’s your will, to live, because he really doubts your*W**ord**s.*”

And he did begin to live. Later, I was telling them a little more about*Dupade*’s*Word**s*and he said, “It’s probably true that there is a*Dupade*, because I could have been killed,” he said, “I’m having to crawl.” He couldn’t walk and had to crawl on the ground.

**Peter,****John,****and the lame man****:***(Acts 3:1-8)*

It was like that when a lame man obeyed John and Peter’s command and walked. You probably know that story. They were going to a meeting in the temple, but you probably already knew that. And they came upon a lame man, who, as you know, couldn’t walk at all, and he could barely crawl. And when they reached him, he said, “Where are you going?” And then he started begging. He said, “Give me some of your money.”

But Peter said: “As if I had any money!”

“But if you don’t have money, then just give me something to eat,” he said.

(I’m just telling you parts of the story. But when I taught it before, my teaching was longer, but I’ll shortened my teaching for you that are here.)

Just as Peter prayed for the lame man, I prayed for Bétidé who you may know as Ayoi, the one who had to crawl also.

Bétidé is generally okay now. He goes to hear*Dupade’s Words*. And for the most part he is happy.

It says that John and Peter said to the lame man, “I’m going to give you something very beautiful.”

You probably know all about **Mateo*in our village who was born crippled. It was the same with that lame man – he was just like Mateo. They said to him, “We’re going to give you something beautiful that we do have. And that beautiful thing is the name of Jesus Christ.”

They were referring to the power of Jesus that could heal people. Jesus had given them his power, so they could heal their fellow people with it. And they took hold of his arm and helped him to stand, and you know what happened next because you’ve heard the story before from those who teach*Dupade*’s*Word**s**.*

This teaching of mine is short, but I’m so thankful that*Dupade*helps us right now. He helps those of you to whom I’m teaching this story.

**I****told this story in Pailón****:**

I told this story in Pailón but Dugúidé said, “That’s always the way it is with some of us Ayoreos, they think they really know how to preach, but they speak in vain.”

**You have surpassed us all:**

You believers here in Rincón have surpassed us all. We’re not following the*Word of**Dupade*in Tobité right now. We continue to meet, and we teach, but only three will show up. But here the place is full of people. There, only four or five at the most come at times. I’m always inviting people to come, but no one responds. They’re full of doubts.

But there are two women in our village that come to hear me. One of them is the one who you used to known as Timingue long ago. She’s faithful to come hear me, and so does Eruidaté. Dapuré is the other one who never fails to show up to hear*Dupade’s Words*. She frequently prays for you here in Rincón at the morning and evening meetings.

**This is all my teaching for you****:**

I know it’s a short message. Sometimes I have longer messages, but this is all my teaching for now. I didn’t want to take too long, that’s why.

But always remember that the name of Jesus is very beautiful. And remember that there is power in his name.*(Philippians 2:9-11)*

*Mateo was Ecarai and Tomasa’s son who was born crippled long ago and he never walked at all.

Dupade – God

Dupade’s Words – Bible, God’s Word

*Ipeyai**–**Rincón de Tigre**-1970*

*Transcribed and translated to English by Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo:
**Ecarai chacate yogome erámi taningai:**Ga chi Dupade chajire dapesudi erãpe nanique ga ueradéjna. Adi jec u uaque gosi. A gosi
chirase dejnai ga chimate ga ueradéjna ome.

Mu Dupade chojninga: “Cáma
yacatique cucha déji,” chojninga. Chaca cuchade. A chaca picadéode. Chaca
esodie, chaca cucha pibosode iji erámi udé údode. Ga chajire dejnai, mu acade
cucha a e sue enga adie cusapo rĩ. Mu chojninga: “Yimate yacade cucha adie mu
cáma gunónique cucha sue to,” chojninga.

**Bajaque cuchisode uje Dupade pésu:**Mu uacarajáyo uje Dupade pésu dejnai gu ayoréode uyoque, cojñone uyóguiji erámi. A chojninga: “A yipésu cuchisode.” Mu cuchisode unojo cuchisode ga que chiraja darárique. Que chiraja cuchárique.

**Ejo ayoré gajnámi uje Dupade pésu nanique:**Mu Dupade chi pesapo dajéode rĩ. Chi chipocásu jnumi enga pésu ayoré gajnámi tiji uje a e uacadúcho jõ, a ore chingo uac, don Juan ore. Chi chimániráse ga chimanipise aja dadode ga chimate edodie jogadode, chimate angoniguinone jogadode, chimate jmainie jogadode rĩ. Ga chi chojninga: “Omi tu ujetiga uecaringuérique déji ajei.” Chi doi ga chubúchu pijnani. Ga e noña ga e ayoré. Mu uacarajáyo uje Dupade pésu ayoré tuné gu nanique ujetiga ayoré tuné doi uru quigode, jetiga tangári.

**Gajnáreque ome Dupade uje gusu ayorérique enga doi ga pésu acoté ome:**Chi chimate. Ga chi chojninga: “Ga gajnáreque jne, uje gusu ayorérique jõ uje que chimo dagutiyamátique,” chojninga. Que chiraja jnani bajai acoté iji cuchisode. Mu chi diga Dupade chijna moi tome yocai, jnani bajai, Adã, ga chi mo ga moi chujé. Dupade chijnime orotabidi pei ga pésu ayoré tiji ga chequé. Ga chi chojningame yocai jnani bajai: “Imate bacoté.” Que chojningame gotique bacoté, mu chojninga, “Yimate bajõra, ujetiga nóna ua enga átayo cucha pisagode aja ñane,” chi chojninga. Jec ũré uje chequedíe déji gu ujetiga ore cháta cuchade aja damaníngo.

**Pangoninguite paritigade chise erámi nanique:**Mu uacarajáyo to uje pangoninguite paritigade chise erámi, enga ore que ore tangári Dupade uruode dirome.

Chi chimate ore ga chojninga:
“Yejnai tuné,” chojninga, “Yejnai tuné. Acho uacajegaitígo iji,” chojninga.
“Yacadie esodie déji dé,” chojninga, “Yacadie ijnaque quedéjnanie adie,” chojninga,
“Picadéode adie, acho,” chojninga. “Mu jé ca acho cucha a uña to,” chojninga,
“uje yarángome uaque.”

Mu que yirajágo írique to mu a Dupade
gãranenie u anírenie, cucha adie uje ayoréode yajo to nanique ujetiga
ayorérique tagu ga toi. Chojninga: “Acho jne. Mu ca acho cucha a chojmara uje
déji uté yejnai tudé gatócoro iji ta,” chojninga.

**Dupade gãrengué chojmára nanique jeta ca ore tagu:**Mu uacamóño uje yocaté, chequé bajé tagu Dupade garengué a enga jec u uje pangoninguite paritigade e chacája yoc. Pitoringai e chacája yoc.

Jé uacadúcho don Carlos
acajnaquéode to uje chojninga: “Pitoringai ipotinguerãque ga pota ayoréode
uyoque ome daquigade.”

Chi ore tagu Dupade gueranenie
cucha ai. Ore irasei. Ore irasique re. Mu chi Satanase chaca ugai ga a pésu re
ga jnacare ueradéjnai. Uacamóño uje yingome uaque diríca. Enga gapúa tuaté que
todo uje chamáñaje ayoré jnacare ueradéjna gu.

**Satanase chetingãre Eba:**E chojninga: “Ebá, Dupade gueranenie déji bei ujetiga acho.”

Mu chojninga: “Dupade gãrengué chojmára ujeta ca yajo to. Dupade chãraque gu.”

Mu chojninga: “Ebá, ise ga
tágape Dupade,” chojninga. “Jecute Dupade que pota ujetiga arajáyo cuchade
jnese,” chojninga. “Dupade charángome uaque. Charatáyime uaque gu ujetiga
arajayo to cuchade,” chojninga.

Enga chi chojningame yocaté,
chequé bajé: “¿Jé go, que agu a?”

Mu chojninga: “Que yagu go. Dupade
chãraque ga que yagu.”

Mu que jno unojo údi gotique.
Obiréjna uje ipotingarãque uné enga jno cuchade údi.

Chi Satanas chingo: “¿Jé
batodóningai udore iji deréjnai? ¿Jé unere ome re adie?” chojninga. “Jé
uacamóño anírone cuchade adie sẽrerãja jnumone ga ipotinguerãcho.”

Chi adie idógosi jnumone
(colgando para abajo) ga ipotinguerẽ ome uaté, yocaté Eba.

Chi chojninga: “Dé udire jõ.
Enga jec yagu jã,” chojninga. Mu chi chojninga: “Mu chi yajo diríca, chi
yitogoi dirome, Dupade chojninga.”

**Satanase cho ãrapataquechoqui iji Dupade:**Mu Satanase chojninga: “Taque Dupade. Uacamóño uje charatáyime uaque gu ujetiga arajáyo cuchade to,” chojninga.

Mu jé ũrepise Dupade uje, jé
ũrepise Satanas uje, a ca yajo cuchade adie unaji ga que yimóngo pajei
gátigárique go. A jé yoquirasique ñane. Que pajei dotáningánique déji yoc. Que
yajéngome yocúringárique choquíbi. Mu yajo unojo ga jé Satanas píode déji to ga
jé u uje yirajágo cucha sijnángone.

Ga chi chojninga: “Qué yagábu gajnerí.”
Chisa a. Chétingãre mu uneréjna. Mu chi chojningame dabai, yocai jnani bajai,
“Boji ti. Agu to á,” chojninga, “Enga jec ũré uneréjna. A Dupade chãra yoque,”
chojninga, “Uje chãraque diríca,” chojninga.

**Uje ore tagu Dupade gãrengué a, enga quene ore chiraja uje choquíbi ore:**Ore tagu. Mu chi ore chojninga, “Jé go uje choquíbi yoc,” ore chojninga.

Mu abai chi chajire re to enga chojninga: “Jec ũré, choquíbi yoc,” chi ore chojninga.

Ore poté tudaque cucha a u uje ore chiraja uje
choquíbi ore; ore chiraja pajegátigade.

Ga chi chojningame dacoté: “Yico a. Yipésabu yocoitígo.”

A ore pésu doidode udé ome cucha
acarane, acarãque ejnúmeone udore. Ujnoro quedéjnanie uje uacamóño ujnoro
quedéjnanie ojodie uje ejnúmecho. Ga ore chugája ñane enga ore pésu doidode
iji.

Mu chi guedé jno enga ore
chudute Dupade pijnane. Chi chojninga: “¡Adã́, qué yibágui uaque é!”
chojninga.

Mu
chojningame dacoté: “Boji ti. Yico.
Dupade e di,” chojninga.

Chi ore doi poridie uéchade enga
e ore chacose re. Enga chi a ore chojninga: “A ca Dupade chimo yoque gajnerĩ.
Chamáñaje acoco yoque gu.”

Uacamóño uje a yacose yu una
uéchai enga uaque gosi que chimo yu. Mu Dupade ¡cho jetiga ca chimo uaque go! Adi
jé u uyoque uje a gosi pógu date gajnarétigo mu Dupade u uje chimo cuchade,
cucha pipoguiédode.

**Dupade chajna ore aja ore acoyapídi:**Chi jé ore chajo rája dacoyapídode enga, mu diga Dupade chajnapúsu ore aja.

Chi chojninga: “¿Dupade ta uque e?” chi chojninga. “Jé go uje a Dupade u que,” chojninga, “Jé go, jé Dupade a u,” chojninga.

“Yugúcoi ne,” chojninga, mu
cachi ore urúo. Cachi ore urúo uje chi gaidi tu ujetiga ca Dupade chiraja ore
anírone, ore ipesudode cucha gajnarécho. Ga chiese ore tṍra re ome Dupade.

Jecute ore chojninga: “Jé go, a Dupade
u,” ore chojninga.

Chojninga, “Ugúcoi ne,”
chojninga.

Enga yirajágo uje a bajéngome
cuchade gu uje pisijnaringuei chisa ua enga e bajéngome cuchade.

Chi ore chojninga: “Ugúcoi ne,”
chojninga. Chi chojninga: “Ugúcoi ne mu yacóco yoque que uje choquíbi yoque
gu.”

Mu chojninga: “Ise ga e acho
yarengué cucha a. Jecute uacapócho uaque,” chojninga.

Mu chojningame Dupade: “Dupade,
e yagu. Berané chequé chisiome yu ga yagu to,” chojninga, “Uje chojninga, Adã,
agu to.”

Mu jé ũrepise ujetiga bacoté ca
changureta Dupade enga a jé ca áta ua aja ipesutígo cucha gajnarécho ujeta
angureta Dupade. Bacoté ujetiga ca changureta Dupade enga jé ca arate uaté aja
ipesutígo cucha gajnarécho.

Jecute uné jnani bajai uné a ca
tangári dacoté uaté enga a ca Dupade chisírasome pitoringai tome to gotique.

Jé basa ga ujetiga bacoté
changureta Dupade enga jé ca ajocase úringai Dupade irajato gai. Enga ujetiga Dupade
anguretato ua, enga ca áta ua aja babai ipesudode cucha gajnarécho,
angoninguite paritigade.

Jecute chi ore chojningame,
unojo ore: “Yajo cucha adie, bãrangué. Jecute yipójo yógome ua,” ore chojninga.

**Dupade chijócãre ore iji dajutícabia:**Mu chi chojninga: “Yiraja. Uacacho,” chojninga, “Boyo enga acayoi tõniñeque tiji yejnai. Ca acayopo aja ne jne rĩ,” chojninga.

Ore jno ga ore cho dáyugu rágaja
dajesúode. Ore educacho aja dajesúode uje Dupade cha ore.

Mu Dupade que cha ore go, mu qué
pesapo dajéode ome ore to jne enga qué chijnapo ore aja daniri rĩ. Chi
chojninga: “Jé ca boyo jne maringa,” chojninga, “mu a ca achopo uacabotique
bisideque jne rĩ,” chojninga.

Uacamóño uje Dupade chisi ome
cucha adie ome ore ujetiga ore tagu ga cáma ore chisa dejórigo ujetiga ore cho
paráguiji usodi ujetiga ore taque enga ore moi damochádode.

**Dupade cho darúja ome ore enga chisi ome pitoringai ome ore, jé aja pisaque etógode ore:**Mu chi chojninga, cho darúja ome yocai jnani bajai. Jec u uje yitogoi ga e u Dupade uruode.

Chi chojninga: “A qué aque jne mu a ejna baguesi ome cucha pisagode bajaque,” chojninga.

Enga jec ũrepise Dupade uje
ayoré casodicaite, que ore taque bisideque go. Ise ga uacuñeque chiraja to uje
ayoré casodicaite uyoque que yajo bisideque go. Jé ũré yu uje que ayorérique
déji siñeque ujetiga taque bisideque. Ayoré casodicaite ore taque jne mu qué
ore báguiji erámi. Ga unojo u uje Dupade cho daru ome yoque. Que chisiapo ome
yoque cucha pibosode rĩ jeta cucha pibosode diapo iji yocajei bisideque
gotique rĩ.

Uje cucha pibosode u yocade ore.
Yacorenie chiraja uje yujégo enga ore chayome yoque ga que ore diguiji yocajei
go. Jé basa ga a que yipéco cucha gajnarétique tiji ome Dupade nanique, enga
yacorenie, yocade diguiji yocajei enga yajo. Enga ayoré casodicaite gosi
báguiji erámi. Que chimo cuténique míraque ga chaguhéode ujuya ga cho jõ. Cho
caru ome erámi isagode.

Jé basa ga cojñone unojo u to.
Cojñone, ayoréode que taque bisideque go. Ore chejna daguesi ome darosónique cojñoque
iroquérigo, jneque tagu to mu (más tarde comerán).

Ga unojo u uje Dupade cho daru
ome jnani bai ga uruode unojo uje chojninga: “A gajnetui yore babai,”
chojninga, “quigade eti baque to,” chojninga.

**Dupade cho daru aja chequé bajé Eva:**Enga cho daru aja anire, yocãrapié chequé bajé.

“Iraja,” chojninga, “uje
báyipie, bajei gaidode a babai u,” chojninga, “Bapotiguéode gajnengóri tu
babai,” chojningame. “Mu a uacatígo cuse jne,” chojninga.

E cho darú ome yocaté chequé
bajé to. “A uacatique jne, mu qué baraja ũrosoringuéone,” chojninga, “Baraja
ũrosoringuéone enga que toc ujeta batique tocádiji ua bisideque,” chojninga.

Ga unojo uje yirajágo uje Dupade
guerãtique cucha gajnaréngone ome yocayode jnani bajade uje cho daru aja ore.

**Dupade cho daru aja erámi:**Quigade ga cho daru aja erámi. Chojningame erámi: “A bojnatígo cuse jne,” chojninga. Dupade guejnai chi gusu anírone nanique, acade cucha, cucha a. Que cucha ojnatique déji to. Mu catáguedie déji ayoréode yoquejnane. Mu Dupade cho daru aja jnumi ga chojninga: “A bojnatígo cuse jne,” chojninga. “A ojnacúco,” chojninga.

Ga quigade uacamóño uje que
ayorérique guejnatique déji siñeque jeta ca catágueraque déji. A ca déji
siñeque jetiga ca ojnacúcoraque déji. Ojnacúcodie déji erámi jnese uje Dupade
cho daru aja erámi.

Enga unojo u uje yirajágo uje
yocayode jnani bajade ore angoninguite paritigade u uje Dupade chetaque yoque.

**Dupade cho darúja ore, mu qué chijnóra ore:**Mu quigade unojo enga Dupade chi chucué dajéode iji gatajnoquei. Chojninga: “Yipesudi casodicaite uje yo yuru aja ore, yijnorapise ore ome yu,” chojninga. “Yijnorapise ore ome yiquigade ujetiga ore nóna yu,” chojninga.

Chojninga: “Yi jne enga, a yiroque
yujnacari Jesute aja jnumi te jne enga chingo ore jne uje qué yijnorapise ore,”
chojninga.

Enga jec ũré uje Jesute chi di
iji jnumi nanique mu yoquisijnaringuéone chinguírapo iji jnumi te rĩ . Enga
yoquisijnaringuéone u uje jnani bajade ore chajo aja curesei. Enga unojo u uje Dupade,
yoquetetigade qué chajo raja ome Dupade. Enga yoquisijnaringuéone, disi ejode
uyoque, jé aja Jesuse irabade ore, ore chajo aja curesei. Mu unojo quigade enga
Jesus ecapo iji dáringai toque rĩ enga e uacarajáyo to uje que Dupade cha yoque
gotique ome dajnoratiguei.*(1 Pedro 3:18)*

*Ecarai – Rincón de Tigre, Bolivia – 1970**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Ecarai acajnaquéode nanique*
english:
*Dupade chajire dapesudi erãpe ga ueradéjna*

**Ecarai teaches us about the beginning of the world:**

*Dupade*looked at the earth he had made, and it was beautiful.

That’s how you and I are, too. When someone makes a garden, after it’s finished he looks it over and finds it beautiful.

But*Dupade*said: “I haven’t planted a garden yet.” So, he planted things. He planted the*picadéode*trees. He planted the*esodie*trees. He planted things for food all over the world. And he looked at his garden, and saw plants coming up and producing fruit. But he said to himself: “I’ve finished planting the things in my garden, but what is lacking is people to eat it!”

**God made the animals first:**

But you know how it really was.*Dupade*made his garden for us humans that inhabit the earth, Ayoreos and Cojñone. He said: “I’ll make the animals.” He did, but these animals that he made were just animals and they couldn’t speak. They couldn’t reason.

**Afterwards God made a man in his image:**

But then*Dupade*made up his mind to make something else. He took some of the dirt and he formed it and made a special*man-like thing*from it, as you have probably already heard from don Juan and others. He kept perfecting it: he formed a place for it’s eyes, for ears, and for hands. And then he said: “Now it would be good if it had*life*in it.” So, he blew into it’s lips. And it was changed from a figure made of dirt into a human being. But you know that*Dupade*made this human being so that it would do what he asked it to do, and that it would obey him.

**God saw it wasn’t good for the man to be alone so he made a wife for him:**

He said: “It wouldn’t be good for this person to be alone and not have the benefit of a companion.” He knew none of the animals could be a wife for the man he had made. But then,*Dupade*put the man to sleep, that first man Adam, and soon he was sound asleep.*Dupade*took a piece of his rib and from it made a woman. And then he said to our father, the first man: “I’ve just finished making you a wife.” No, he didn’t call her a wife, but said, “I have just finished making a companion for you, someone who will stay with you and you will help each other with your work.” It’s true that women were made to help their husbands.

**Disobedience entered the world:**

But you must know that disobedience was to enter the world, and the people were not going to listen to*Dupade*.

After they were made,*Dupade*took them into his garden and said to them: “This is my garden. Eat whatever you want from it. Here’s the*esodie*I planted,” he said. “Here’s the palm fruit called*ijnaque quedéjnanie*,” he said. “Eat some of this fruit from the*picadéode*trees,” he said. “But do not eat this other fruit, I forbid you to eat it.”

But we don’t know the name of the fruit that*Dupade*told them not to eat; the fruit that, if we humans should eat it, we would die.*Dupade*said to them: “Eat. But don’t eat this one fruit that is in the middle of my garden.”

**One of the fruits in****Dupade****’s garden was forbidden:**

But you know that our mother, the first woman, ate the forbidden fruit, and that is how disobedience entered us humans. Death also came into us when she ate the fruit.

You heard the teachings of don Carlos when he said: “Death lusts after us humans and wants us to follow it to our demise.”

And so, they ate the fruit that*Dupade*gave to them and they were happy. They were happy until Satan entered the snake and changed himself into a handsome young man, like I told you about yesterday. And that young woman didn’t fear him, because this was a good-looking young man.

**Satan tempts Eve:**

Now he speaks to her and says: “Eve, here’s some of the things that*Dupade*gave for you to eat.”

But she said: “There’s one fruit that*Dupade*has forbidden us to eat.*Dupade*has forbidden it and that’s why we can’t eat it.”

But Satan said: “Eve, I think that*Dupade*is lying to you. It’s because he doesn’t want you to know everything. That’s why he told you not to eat it. He’s holding it back from you, so that you won’t know things.”

And then he said to our mother, the first woman, “Why don’t you eat it?”

But she said: “I’m not going to eat it.*Dupade*told me not to eat it.”

But she couldn’t keep resisting the temptation to eat the forbidden fruit. It’s obvious that her cravings were leading her forward toward the fruit.

And Satan said: “Why are you so afraid all the time? Won’t that fruit taste good?”

Now, you know, when fruit is very ripe it becomes very appealing!”

The fruit was just hanging there, tempting our mother Eve.

She said: “Here they are. And I am going to eat them.” But then she said: “But if we eat this fruit, we’ll die just like*Dupade*said.”

**Satan calls****Dupade****a****big****liar:**

But Satan said: “*Dupade*is a liar. Can’t you see he is holding the fruit back from you because you will know things like he does if you eat it.”

But*Dupade*was truthful, and in one sense so was Satan, for if we wouldn’t have eaten that fruit then we wouldn’t have been ashamed. We’d be happy with each other. We wouldn’t get anger. We wouldn’t have been embarrassed of being naked. But we did eat it, and Satan found a way to enter us and we learned to sin.
